column2:
Type

(lack of type indicates no type to be used in TEI)

This is a legacy of our early TEI. We keep this information and keep encoding it in new documents for the sake of consistency. It has no searchability or relevance and wasn't implemented rigorously. What I mean is that categories and labels aren't very good, and because of the sprawl of annotations did not end up being applied consistently.
column4:
Name

This is the title/label of the one liner and should match the text preceding the colon in the write-up itself.
column5:
Write-Up

The text preceding the colon in this column should match the text in the name/label column.
column6:
Sources & Notes

Wherever possible, the information in this column should be in the format of a citation rather than a single link. Full/formal citations are not necessary, but the goal should always be to allow someone else to follow the documentary/digital trail back to the item, whether or not its digital location has changed.

column5: description
Active: a steamer ship used by the US Coast Survey between 1852-61. It was one of the first two US Survey ships on the west coast.
column6: Cloud, John. "The US Coast Survey in the Civil War." Office of Coast Survey. June 2011. http://www.nauticalcharts.noaa.gov/history/CivilWar/docs/The_U_S_Coast_Survey_in_the_Civil_War_report.pdf

column5: Albatross: an American ship, captained by New Englander Nathan Winship, that partnered with the Russian American Company. While Americans provided the ship, the RAC provided skilled Alaska Native hunters. The Albatross's route ran along the Californian coast, between Russian-owned Alaska and California, and it sailed in the Pacific in the same years as the Il’mena.
column6: Ogden, Adele. The California Sea Otter Trade 1784-1848. Berkeley and Los Angeles: University of California Press, 1941.

column5: Alert: a three-masted, 398-ton ship owned by Bryant, Sturgis & Company of Boston. During the nineteenth century, the Alert traded on the California coast. In May 1841, Captain William Dane Phelps wrote that he had contact with a Nicoleño known as Black Hawk at San Pedro. Phelps also noted that another sea captain, Thomas Robbins, said that he had previously taken two Nicoleñas to San Pedro.

Arctic Star: a ship sailed along the Californian coast by Scott O’Dell with his wife and two other men. O’Dell’s book The Cruise of the Arctic Star, published in 1973, was inspired by this voyage. The novel recounts his trip along the coast while discussing California’s history.
column6: "Cruise of the Arctic Star." Scott O'Dell. Accessed May 03, 2016. http://www.scottodell.com/Pages/CruiseoftheArcticStar.aspx.

column5: Avon: a ship involved in the sea otter trade along the California coast, beginning in 1817. It was renamed Trinidad in 1842.
column6: Ogden, Adele. The California Sea Otter Trade 1784-1848. Berkeley and Los Angeles: University of California Press, 1941.

column5: Bolivar Liberator: a 224-ton brig built by Alpheus Basil Thompson, an American who became a naturalized Mexican citizen; co-owned by Thompson, Farnham and Co., and John Coffin Jones; and captained by Gorham Hayden Nye in the early 1830s. The Bolivar Liberator engaged in coastal trade. In 1832, it carried horses to the Sandwich Islands (Hawaii). It became involved in the otter trade beginning in 1834, with the ship’s crew (including George Nidever) hunting otters on San Nicolas and San Clemente Islands in 1843-44, a time overlapping with the Lone Woman's isolation. The brig was renamed Oajaca in 1843 and began sailing under the Mexican flag.
column6: Ogden, Adele. The California Sea Otter Trade 1784-1848. Berkeley and Los Angeles: University of California Press, 1941. See also April 2018 Shore to Sea lecture at CINP and compiled research notes from Linda Bentz (Chinese Historical Society of Southern California).

column5: Charon: a ship owned by the P.T. Jackson, of Boston. During the War of 1812, the Charon was captured by the British ship Cherub at the Sandwich Islands (Hawaii) in 1814. Some newspaper accounts incorrectly reported that it was the Charon, captained by Whittemore, that left the hunting party responsible for the massacre of the massacre of the Nicoleños on San Nicolas Island in 1811. In reality, it was the Il'mena, a Russian American Company ship, in the year 1814.
column6: Adele Ogden, California Sea Otter Trade, 164-65

column5: Convoy: a 135-ton brig that was owned by Josiah Marshall of Boston in 1824 and then by John Coffin Jones in the early 1830s. It engaged in the Northwest and Pacific trade for many years. Richard Henry Dana describes an 1836 visit with its crew on the California Coast in his Two Years Before the Mast. At the time, many members of the otter-hunting crew were Tlingit.
column6: Mary Malloy, "Boston Men" on the Northwest Coast: The American maritime Fur Trade, 1788-1844 (Limestone Press, 1998).  List of John Coffin Jones vessels, from Ogden MerchantTraders, provided by Linda Bentz.

column5: Cora: the 16 1/95 ton burthen schooner George Nidever brought to San Nicolas Island with hunting groups in 1852 and 1853. During the summer 1853 trip, the Lone Woman made herself visible to one member of Nidever’s hunting party, Carl Dittman, and when the crew sailed for Santa Barbara at the end of the month’s hunt, the Lone Woman joined them. Nidever had purchased the schooner together with two other men, James Alden and Thomas H. Stevens (lieutenants in the U.S. Coast Survey), in San Francisco in 1852. They used the vessel for a variety of economic pursuits.
column6: Chinese Historical Society presentation by Linda Bentz; forthcoming article co-authored by Linda and Susan Morris.  Maritime license for the Cora (in Linda Bentz research folder).  See Shore-to-Sea lecture at CINP, April 2018 for photo of maritime licence

column5: Euphemia: a 133-ton brig built in Dundee, Scotland that sailed between 1834-50 under British, Hawaiian, and American flags. The Euphemia made voyages to Valparaiso (Chile), Callao (Peru), Hawaii, and Hong Kong, and it spent much of its time engaged in coastal California trade. The brig is connected to the Lone Woman’s story in two ways. In 1843, Carl Dittman joined the Euphemia’s crew in Liverpool for a commercial voyage chartered by merchants James Scott and John Wilson; although the ship was declared unseaworthy once it reached Hawaii, in 1844, Dittman joined the ship’s cargo on board the Juanita, which continued on to Alta California. Dittman then settled in California, and in 1853, he would be part of the otter hunting party that brought the Lone Woman to Santa Barbara. Meanwhile, the unseaworthy Euphemia was sold to William Heath Davis, Eliab Grimes, and Hiram Grimes. In 1846, under this ownership, the Euphemia left Hawaii and anchored near San Nicolas Island to off-load most of its cargo in a strategy designed to avoid tariffs upon docking in California. Crew member John Young was left behind on San Nicolas Island to guard the cargo; as a result, he spent two months on San Nicolas Island during the years of the Lone Woman’s isolation there. Finally, in 1849, the Euphemia was sold by Heath to the town council of San Francisco, to be used as a prison. It served this purpose and simultaneously housed the mentally ill, effectively becoming the first asylum in the region.
column6: David W. Forbes, Hawaiian National Bibliography 1780-1900 vol. 1 (Ann Arbor: Honolulu University of Hawaii Press, 1999), 438; Adele Ogden, Trading Vessels on the California Coast, 1786–1848. Unpublished manuscript, 1979. (Bancroft Library, University of California, Berkeley, 1254-56); William Heath Davis Collection, Box 3, Folder 111, The Huntington Library, San Marino, California; James P. Delgado, “Gold Rush Jail: The Prison Ship Euphemia” California History 60, no. 2 (1981): 134-41.  See also Linda Bentz’s “Capsule History of Events for the Brig Euphemia.”

column5: Goliah: a steam towboat built in New York and in service between 1849-89. Also known as the Defender, its home port was San Francisco.
column6: Porter, Daniel, and Dave Wright. "Goliah (1849)." Tugboat Information. Accessed May 09, 2016. http://www.tugboatinformation.com/tug.cfm?id=1701.

column5: Griffon: a brig built in 1824 in Medford, Massachusetts, and initially purchased by Bryant and Sturgis, a Boston shipping business that traded in lumber, tea, opium, and sugar, among other goods. The Griffon operated on the Northwest Coast for five years. In 1837, it was under the ownership of John Coffin Jones.
column6: Fromm, James R. "Early Sailing Ships Trading on the Northwest Coast of America 1788-1837." Early Sailing Ships Of The Pacific Northwest. Accessed May 09, 2016. http://www.3rd1000.com/history3/Early%20Ships/Early%20Ships%20of%20the%20Pacific%20Northwest.htm

column5: Hassler: a US Coast Survey ship designed to chart the US Pacific Coast. It mapped the coast of California and its islands while in service between 1871-95.
column6: "The Hassler and the Triumphs and Problems of Innovation." National Marine Sanctuaries. August 07, 2007. http://sanctuaries.noaa.gov/maritime/expeditions/hassler/innovative.html.

column5: Helvetius: a trading ship built in 1804 by Frenchman Stephen Girard and then converted into a whaler. The Helvetius was known for the hand-carved bust of its French philosopher namesake, which was positioned at the helm of the ship. It made its first whaling voyage to the Pacific and Virgin Islands in 1832. On its return, the Helvetius ran aground on O’ahu island just after midnight on November 10, 1834. The ship was salvaged, but there is no record of the philosopher figurehead being recovered.
column6: Chapin, Helen G. "The Wreck of the Helvetius." The Hawaiian Historical Society. June 14, 2015. Accessed February 13, 2017. https://www.hawaiianhistory.org/time-capsules/disasters/the-wreck-of-the-helvetius/.                 "Voyages of Helvetius (Ship)." American Offshore Whaling Voyages: A Database. 2012. Accessed February 13, 2017. http://nmdl.org/aowv/whvoyage.cfm?VesselNumber=1569.

column5: Il'mena: a two-masted, 200-ton copper-sheathed brig part of the Russian American Company’s sailing ships that visited the California coast on company business. Originally named the Lydia, the ship had been owned by a US sea captain and sold to the Russians in 1813. The following year, in its first voyage under the Russian flag, the Il’mena brought a sea otter hunting crew to San Nicolas Island.
Jennie Griffin: a schooner used for sea otter hunting along the California coast for several decades.
column6: The Seamen's Journal, Volume 32

column5: John Begg: an English trading ship chartered under John Begg & Company, a firm based in Liverpool, England. William Harnell and Hugh McCulloch served as the California agents between 1822-28. After arriving in San Diego in 1822 carrying a shipment of goods from Peru, the brig struck a rock protrusion just off the coast of San Nicolas Island and was grounded in 1824. The rock was subsequently named Begg Rock.
column6: "Begg Rock, San Nicolas Island." Islapedia. Accessed February 9, 2017. http://islapedia.com/index.php?title=Begg_Rock,_San_Nicolas_Island.                                  Gudde, Erwin Gustav. California Place Names: The Origin and Etymology of Current Geographical Names. Los Angeles, CA: University of California Press, 1969. Accessed February 9, 2017.

column5: La Victoria: a ship in Spanish explorer Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo’s fleet. Cabrillo was the first to explore the California coast between 1542-43.
column6: Timmons, Greg. "Juan Rodriquez Cabrillo Biography." Bio. Accessed May 16, 2016. http://www.biography.com/people/juan-rodriquez-cabrillo-090115.

column5: Loriot: an American brig that traded between Spanish California and the Sandwich Islands (Hawaii) in the 1830s. In 1833, it was seized in the San Francisco port and one of its owners, Alpheus B. Thompson, was arrested for illegally hunting otter in Spanish waters along the coast of California (other part owners included Gorham Hayden Nye and John Coffin Jones). By the late 1830s, however, the brig was again trading between the Northwest Coast, California, and Hawaii; John C. Jones was the American Consul in Hawaii at the time.
column6: Ogden, Adele. The California Sea Otter Trade 1784-1848. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1941. Mary Malloy, "Boston Men" on the NW Coast; The American Maritime Fur Trade, 1788-1844, p. 124-25. 3-page note sheet on Gorhan Hayden Nye provided by Linda Bentz (Chinese Historical Society of Southern California)

column5: Louisa: an American merchant ship (bark) owned by John Coffin Jones and involved in the Northwest fur trade along the coast of California in the 1830s.
column6: Ogden, Adele. The California Sea Otter Trade 1784-1848. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1941

column5: Mayflower: a cargo ship that set sail in 1620 and carried 102 passengers from Plymouth, England, across the Atlantic to present-day Massachusetts.

O’Cain: the first contracted "Yankee-Russian" hunting ship. Partnering with the Russian American Company, American Joseph O’Cain provided the ship while the RAC provided skilled Alaska Native hunters. The partnership enabled O’Cain to acquire furs directly, instead of trading for them along the Spanish California coast, and allowed the RAC to hunt in waters further south. The first contract was signed in 1803, and the ship continued to sail under this system until 1816.
column6: Ogden, Adele. The California Sea Otter Trade 1784-1848. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1941

column5: Oajaca: a brig involved in California coastal trade beginning in 1834. Previously named the Bolivar Liberator, it was owned by John Coffin Jones and Alpheus Basil Thompson and used in the sea otter trade. Outgoing cargo on an 1884 sail included beef, potatoes, goat tallow, "joints," and alcohol.
column6: Ogden, Adele. The California Sea Otter Trade 1784-1848. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1941.  Ogden Mercahnt Traders, "Vessels owned by John Coffin Jones" lsit.

column5: Orus: a passenger ship built in 1842. It initially operated on the Shrewsbury River in New Jersey, and was sold to the New York merchant firm Howland and Aspinwall to be used as a tug boat for ships anchored in the river. In 1849 the Orus was purchased by the Nicaragua Steamship Company and transferred to the San Juan River in Nicaragua.
column6: Kemble, John Haskell. The Panama Route 1848-1869. 1943. Reprint, Berkeley: University of California Press, 1990.

column5: Panama: a steamship launched on July 29, 1848 by the Pacific Mail Steamship Company. It sailed between New York and San Francisco through the Panama Route beginning in 1849, until it was transferred to the California coast in 1858 and sold to Holladay and Flint in 1861. The Panama was given to the Mexican government in 1868 as part of a mail contract and renamed the Juarez.
column6: Kemble, John Haskell. The Panama Route 1848-1869. 1943. Reprint, Columbia: University of South Carolina Press, 1990.

column5: Peor es Nada: a 20.5-ton Mexican schooner owned by Jose Joaquín Gómez of Monterey and launched in 1834. Isaac Sparks chartered the ship to hunt sea otter along the California coast in 1834 and 1835. During this time, Charles Hubbard captained the ship. In 1835, the Peor es Nada sailed to San Nicolas Island and returned to San Pedro with the remaining Nicoleños, save one woman who was left behind (she would arrive in Santa Barbara in 1853, on George Nidever's schooner). The Peor es Nada capsized at the entrance to San Francisco Bay in early 1836.

Pilgrim: an American brig built in 1825, owned by the Boston firm Bryant & Sturgis, and engaged in the California trade. The Pilgrim was immortalized by Richard Henry Dana Jr., who recorded his experiences on the vessel in Two Years Before the Mast. The Pilgrim caught fire at sea in 1841, but a replica of the ship was built in 1945 in Dana Point, California, and still makes voyages up and down the California coast as a floating museum.
column6:
"The Brig Pilgrim." Ocean Institute. Accessed February 28, 2017. http://www.ocean-institute.org/post/brig-pilgrim.
Times, Dana Point. "It’s History: The Brig Pilgrim, a Not So Romantic Ending." Dana Point Times. June 10, 2015. Accessed February 28, 2017. http://www.danapointtimes.com/its-history-the-brig-pilgrim-a-not-so-romantic-ending/.
"About the Pilgrim." Ocean Institute. Accessed February 28, 2017. http://www.ocean-institute.org/general-information/about-pilgrim.

column5: Quickstep: a 338-ton steamer that surveyed southern California (including mapping the Channel Islands) in 1851, as part of the U.S. Coast Survey. George Nidever served as the ship's pilot. The steamer was built in Philadelphia in 1850.
column6: Linda Bentz (Chinese Historical Society of Southern California, research notes)

column5: Rasselas: a trading ship involved in the Northwest and China trade in the 1830s. It was owned by Eliab Grims & Company.
column6: Ogden, Adele. The California Sea Otter Trade 1784-1848. Berkeley and Los Angeles: University of California Press, 1941.

column5: San Salvador: the lead ship in Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo’s fleet. Cabrillo was the first European to explore the California coast, between 1542-43.
column6:
Timmons, Greg. "Juan Rodriquez Cabrillo Biography." Bio. Accessed May 16, 2016. http://www.biography.com/people/juan-rodriquez-cabrillo-090115. 
"San Salvador." Maritime Museum of San Diego. Accessed June 23, 2016. http://sdmaritime.org/visit/the-ships/san-salvador/.

column5: Santa Rosa: a passenger ship built in Pennsylvania for the Oregon Steamship Company in 1883. The Santa Rosa was sold in 1897 to the Pacific Coast Steamship Company and ran a regular route between San Francisco and San Diego with various stopping points, including Los Angeles, Redondo, Santa Barbara, Port Hartford, and Santa Cruz Island. It wrecked on July 7, 1911, near Point Arguello while carrying 200 passengers; four died as a result of the crash.
column6: "Santa Rosa (steamer)." Islapedia. February 18, 2016. Accessed September 13, 2016.http://islapedia.com/index.php?title=Santa_Rosa_(steamer).

column5: SS California: the first Pacific Mail steamer to arrive on the West Coast. First launched on May 19, 1848, the steamer’s route passed directly by San Nicolas Island. After 1877, the SS California was converted into a sailing ship and transported cargo. The steamer wrecked off the coast of Peru in 1895.
column6: Bertholdt, Victor M., PhD. "The Pioneer Steamer California." 2010. http://freepages.genealogy.rootsweb.ancestry.com/~npmelton/psc_indx.htm.

column5: SS Colonel Fremont: a ship that traveled between Panama and San Francisco, carrying both passengers and freight. During the 1850s, it was owned by the Pacific Mail Steamship Company.
column6: "Passenger Lists: San Francisco 1800s." The Maritime Heritage Project. Accessed May 07, 2016. http://www.shippassengers.com/passengers/SS-Fremont-1April1852.html.

column5: SS Falcon: a ship owned by the US Mail Steamship Company and in operation between 1848-52. It was then converted into a towboat. The SS Falcon was one of the first ships to run a route to California through the Panama Canal.
column6: Dodsworth, Harry. "Panama Route Ships." Panama Route Ships. February 5, 2005. http://www.theshipslist.com/ships/descriptions/panamafleet.shtml.

column5: Star of Freedom: a wooden sloop built in 1865 in San Francisco and used by the Santa Cruz Island Company (a cattle ranching enterprise). The vessel was sold after more than 25 years of service on the California Channel Islands. The Pacific pirate Oscar Bartels subsequently stole Star of Freedom and sailed to Mexico. Its fate is unknown.
column6: "Star of Freedom." Islapedia. August 17, 2016. Accessed September 13, 2016. http://islapedia.com/index.php?title=Star_of_Freedom.

column5: Undine: the first ship licensed in the United States, on November 5, 1890. It sank among the California Channel Islands exactly one month later.
column6: Morris, Don P., and James Lima. Submerged Cultural Resource Assessment. National Park Service. Nps.gov. February 15, 2002. https://www.nps.gov/orgs/1635/upload/CHIS_159_D109_-54710.pdf.

column5: USS Congress: a ship commissioned in 1841 and assigned to the Mediterranean squadron. During the Mexican-American War, it joined the Pacific Squadron at Monterey Bay and was deployed along the west coast of Mexico; it participated in the battle of Los Angeles. The CSS Virginia ultimately destroyed the USS Congress during the Civil War, when it was assigned to the Atlantic Blockade.
column6: Priolo, Gary P. "USS Congress (IV)." NavSource Online: "Old Navy" Ship Photo Archive. March 20, 2015. http://www.navsource.org/archives/09/86/86259.htm.

column5: USS Edith: an American steamship originally used in the opium trade between British India and China. Sold to the American War Department in 1849, it was used during the Mexican-American War to move Army supplies along the Californian coast. On August 24, 1849, having encountered a heavy fog, it ran aground and wrecked in the Santa Barbara Channel, near San Nicolas island.
column6: Ruhge, Justin M. "Wreck of the USS Edith, August 24, 1849." California Naval History. February 08, 2016. http://www.militarymuseum.org/Edith.html.

column5: USS Portsmouth (II): an American sloop-of-war launched in 1843. In service during the Mexican-American War and the American Civil War, it not only engaged in general surveillance and disruption of the slave trade but undertook several transoceanic voyages. The USS Portsmouth was decommissioned from the Navy and sold in 1915.
column6: "USS Portsmouth." Hoboken Historical Museum : Online Collections. Accessed February 15, 2017. http://hoboken.pastperfectonline.com/bysearchterm?keyword=USS%2BPortsmouth. SOURCE HAS DISAPPEARED

column5: USS Vandalia: a Navy ship first launched in 1828. It was stationed off the coast of South America to protect merchant ships until it was decommissioned in 1831. The ship was decommissioned and recommissioned several times, assigned to the Pacific Squadron and various other duties along the coast until the outbreak of the Civil War brought it back to the South Atlantic Blockading Squadron in 1861. The USS Vandalia was decommissioned for the last time in 1863.
column6: "Naval History and Heritage Command." Vandalia I (Sloop-of-War). October 21, 2015. https://www.history.navy.mil/research/histories/ship-histories/danfs/v/vandalia-i.html.

column5: Volunteer: an American merchant ship (bark) involved in the Pacific trade in the 1830s. It was owned by John Coffin Jones and utilized for the transport of both hide & tallow and otter pelts in California.
column6: Ogden, Adele. The California Sea Otter Trade 1784-1848. Berkeley and Los Angeles: University of California Press, 1941.

column5: Washington: a schooner owned by the Boston merchant firm Marshall & Wildes. It was involved in the Pacific trade during the 1820s.
column6: Ogden, Adele. The California Sea Otter Trade 1784-1848. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1941.

column5: Waverly: a brig involved in the Northwest trade between Hawaii and California. William Goodwin Dana was associated with the vessel.
column6: Ogden, Adele. The California Sea Otter Trade 1784-1848. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1941.

column5: Winfield Scott: an American steamship built in 1850 by the Pacific Mail Steamship Company. Running the Panama route beginning in 1852, the steamship carried passengers and mail from New York to San Francisco. However, when traveling from San Francisco to Panama in 1853, the ship ran aground on Anacapa Island. Scavengers and treasure hunters, hoping to recover undiscovered gold from its last shipment, visited the wreckage so frequently that it was eventually listed on the National Register of Historic Places in order to protect the remaining ruins.
column6:
"Shipwrecks: The Winfield Scott." National Parks Service. Accessed February 16, 2017. https://www.nps.gov/chis/learn/historyculture/winfieldscott.htm.
"The wreck of the 1850s California Gold Rush steamship Winfield Scott." National Marine Sanctuaries. August 01, 2012. Accessed February 16, 2017. http://sanctuaries.noaa.gov/maritime/expeditions/ws_2005.html.
Kemble, John Haskell. The Panama Route 1848-1869. 1943. Reprint, University of California Press, 1990.

column5: Baikal: formerly the American brig Arab, this 215-ton vessel was purchached by the Russian American Company and was operating on the coast as late as 1839. It transported supplies, including salt, to the Russian colonies and brought Russian goods to Chile in 1829.
column6: Ogden, Adele. The California Sea Otter Trade 1784-1848. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1941.

column5: Big Loafer: a sloop used for fishing in California’s coastal waters, including in and around the Channel Islands, between the 1890s-1904. It was used at least occasionally to transport Chinese abalone fishermen to the islands.
column6: References in California newspapers from 1893, 1901, 1904.  See Linda Bentz vessel notes

column5: Crusader: a 113-ton brig that sailed between Hawaii, California, and Alaska in 1832, chartered by John Coffin Jones. The Crusader left Honolulu in March, took aboard Kaigani Haida hunters at Norfolk Sound, and hunted otter off the California coast and around Cerros Island during the summer, before returninig to Honoulu in September.
column6: Gast, Contentious Consul: A Biography of John Coffin Jones (1976): 126.

column5: Fama: owned by Alpheus B. Thompson and John Coffin Jones, the Fama engaged in trade between California and Hawaii. Gorham Hayden Nye frequently served as its captain. Outgoing cargo for an 1844 journey included hides, soap, horses, goatskins, deerskins, lumber, and shingles. The ship wrecked on February 26, 1846, near Santa Barbara.
column6: Journal of a Sea Captain's Wife: 1841-1845, edited by Doyce Nunis.  "Vessels Owned by John Coffin Jones," Ogden Merchant Traders (from Linda Bentz)

column5: Genoa: a schooner (sometimes called the Genova or Geneva) used for fishing and pleasure trips along the coast.
column6: LA Times July 20, 1895; Santa Barbara Morning Press April 17, 1900

column5: Harriet Blanchard: a 62-ton schooner owned by John Coffin Jones and sailing under a US flag. It engaged in Pacific trade, traveling between California, Hawaii, and some Pacific islands. Cargo included hide & tallow, otter furs, and horses.
column6: Ogden, Trading Vessels on the California Coast, 1786–1848. Unpublished manuscript, 1979. (Bancroft Library, University of California, Berkeley, 1254-56) 603; 632. Ross H. Gast, Contentious Consul: A Biography of John Coffin Jones (1976): 127-28)

column5: Hattie: a sloop built in San Francisco in 1889 with home port in Wilmington, California. It measured 38.5 (length) x 12.6 (breadth) x 8.5 (depth) with a gross register tonnage of 12.96 and net register tonnage of 12.42.
column6: Linda Bentz vessel list. LMVUS 1892

column5: Ida: a sloop built in San Francisco in 1872. It had a gross register tonnage of 15.50 and net register tonnage of 14.74.
column6: Linda Bentz vessel list. LMVUS 1880, 1884, 1885

column5: Isabella: a sloop, with home ports variously in Santa Barbara, San Diego, and San Francisco. It wrecked at San Miguel Island in 1886. The Isabella's gross register tonnage was 12.80 and net register tonnage 12.16.
column6: Linda Bentz vessel list. LMVUS 1883, 1884, 1885, 1886

column5: Juanita: a hermaphrodite brig owned by California shipmasters John Wilson and James Scott beginning in 1844; it engaged in coastal trade and employed a multiethnic crew that included native Hawaiians.

Juanita: a hermaphrodite brig owned by California shipmasters John Wilson and James Scott beginning in 1844; it engaged in coastal trade and employed a multiethnic crew that included native Hawaiians.
Los Angeles: a steamer built in San Francisco in 1874. It brought passengers to Santa Cruz Island throughout the early 1890s.
column6: Santa Cruz Island Company invoices, 1890-1894--see Linda Bentz vessel records

column5: Matinee: 37-ton schooner with home port of San Francisco.
column6: Linda Bentz vessel record

Pearl: schooner built in Benica, California with home port in San Francisco. It wrecked on Anacapa Island in 1891.
column6: Linda Bentz vessel list; LMVUS 1887

column5: Phoebe: a 36-gun British Royal Navy ship engaged in the War of 1812 on the United States’ west coast. Together with the Cherub, the Phoebe captured the American ships Essex and Essex-Junior at Valparaiso, Chile in 1814. The Cherub also intercepted the American merchant ship Charon, with Isaac Whittemore as captain, near Hawaii in 1814.
column6: Susan Morris vessel notes

column5: Rover: a schooner owned by Luis Antonio Arguello and engaged in otter hunting on the California coast in the early nineteenth century.

column5: Santa Barbara: a schooner built in San Francisco in 1891 and owned by John Coffin Jones; it sailed between the Channel Islands and mainland, transporting abalone, shells, and other items.

column5: Senator: a 1012-ton steamer built in New York City in 1848. It was transported sheep between Santa Cruz Island and the California mainland in the 1870s.
